payload.evidence_summary: Stored DLC evidence is sparse but clear that this is a story DLC for Starfield: it adds new locations, technologies, weapons, and enemy encounters, and does not indicate a new standalone mode, genre shift, or different gameplay structure. Per the DLC guidance, the parent game's richer stored gameplay evidence should be treated as primary. Since the DLC is additive and non-contradictory, inheriting Starfield's existing core gameplay identity is appropriate.

Stored evidence is too thin to support a reliable taxonomy placement. There is no description or gameplay text in the DB, only broad source labels indicating puzzle, strategy, roguelike, and single-player. Those labels do not establish a concrete gameplay identity within the allowed archetypes or support a fingerprint with sufficient specificity.
payload.rejection_reason: Insufficient gameplay evidence; only high-level genre/theme labels are present, with no descriptive text to determine core structure, perspective, session shape, or mechanics.

payload.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently describe a single-player fantasy action RPG built around returning to a hometown, exploring the surrounding area, helping townspeople, real-time slash-and-dodge combat with a machete, companion-assisted fights, ingredient gathering, cooking, and bond-building. That supports an action-RPG/quest-exploration identity more than farming sim or pure life sim, with the strongest fit in this taxonomy being open_world_fantasy_action_rpg, while the relationship/story emphasis gives it some jrpg_story_rpg overlap. Evidence is solid on core loop and tone, but weaker on exact camera/presentation and on whether the world is fully open versus hub-and-region structured.

Stored evidence consistently describes a short, low-stress single-player fishing sim with handcrafted art, light narrative choices, and a relaxing tone. However, the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetypes do not provide a strong fit for a narrative fishing experience of this kind. It does not strongly align with sports_sim, life_sim, or any combat-, platforming-, strategy-, or RPG-led archetype, and forcing a match would misrepresent its gameplay identity.
payload.rejection_reason: No strong-fit archetype in the allowed taxonomy for a low-stress narrative fishing sim; evidence supports the game identity, but not a valid taxonomy_v2 archetype classification.

payload.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions repeatedly identify Prop Sumo as a 'party fighting game' centered on PvP matches where players 'beat up your friends,' score highest, and fight in 1v1 or free-for-all battles. Core play is melee brawling with chains of light/heavy attacks, charges, dynamic arenas, items, training to master moves and combos, and round-end unlock progression. The stored taxonomy candidate strongly favored traditional_fighter, with identity-driving evidence for win_matches, duel_focused combat, and match_competition. Party-game flavor is clear, but the richer evidence points to a competitive melee match fighter first.
